content.body: This document exists to define UX modes and valid transitions between them. Use it when a corpus needs to distinguish existing, preserved, desired, ideal, temporary, proposed, pending, deprecated, rejected, or unknown UX.

content.title: Modes
body: `current` means behavior appears to exist now. It is discovery input, not automatic implementation authority.
`confirmed-current` means current behavior has been intentionally confirmed for preservation.
`desired` means approved target behavior that implementation should follow.
`ideal` means best-known UX direction that may inform future work but is not automatically approved for the current pass.
`intermediate` means a temporary UX state used during staged rollout, migration, or refactor.
`proposed` means recommended UX that has not yet been confirmed.
`pending-confirmation` means the UX decision is blocked on product/user confirmation.
`deprecated` means behavior is intentionally being phased out.
`rejected` means behavior or proposal was considered and explicitly not chosen.
`unknown` means behavior is not yet discovered, derivable, or confirmed.

content.title: Valid Transition Patterns
body: `current -> confirmed-current -> desired` is used when existing behavior is intentionally preserved as the desired target.
`current -> proposed -> pending-confirmation -> desired` is used when an agent recommends a change that needs confirmation before it can govern implementation.
`current -> intermediate -> desired` is used when a staged refactor or rollout requires temporary UX behavior before the final target.
`current -> ideal -> desired` is used when product exploration identifies an ideal direction and then narrows it into an approved target.
`current -> deprecated` is used when existing behavior should be phased out.
`unknown -> current` is used when investigation discovers what exists.
`unknown -> pending-confirmation` is used when investigation cannot resolve the behavior and product intent is needed.

content.title: Implementation Target Rule
body: Implementation should target `desired` or confirmed `intermediate` only.
Implementation should not target raw `current`, `ideal`, `proposed`, `pending-confirmation`, `rejected`, or `unknown` UX.
If work must proceed while a behavior is unresolved, route unresolved behavior into pending decisions and limit implementation to confirmed or safely separable areas.

content.title: Mapping Rule
content.body: Use current-to-desired mappings to explain transitions. Do not rely on mode labels alone to communicate whether behavior is preserved, changed, added, removed, split, merged, or blocked.
